region_of_origin: South Asia (Indian subcontinent); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a naan bread entry (FOODON_00002683) but does not have a specific entry for mini naan served with dip; closest_parent used for the bread component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers flat bread as a category; no specific code exists for mini naan with dip as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code found for mini naan with dip as a composite food item.

main_ingredients: dipping sauce (chutney, raita, or butter chicken sauce)

serving.description: 1 serving of mini naan with dip (approximately 3–4 mini naan pieces with dipping sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of 3 to 4 mini naan pieces (approximately 100–110 g of bread) accompanied by approximately 40–50 g of dipping sauce such as mint chutney, raita, or butter chicken sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, plain (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) combined with estimated dip component from USDA FoodData Central (Yogurt-based raita / mint chutney)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Naan per-100g macros derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (plain naan / tandoori roti values). Dip component (assumed yogurt-based raita or mint chutney at ~25–30% of total weight) estimated from USDA FoodData Central yogurt and chutney entries. Composite weighted average calculated at approximately 70% naan bread and 30% dip by weight. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for this restaurant-style dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; naan bread GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 is based on naan bread (white wheat flour, tandoor-baked) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Mini naan is compositionally equivalent to standard naan; the dip component (yogurt-based raita or mint chutney) contributes negligible additional glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (71 × 63.75) / 100 ≈ 45.4, based on 150 g serving with 42.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~570 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 mini naan pieces and choose low-sodium dips such as plain yogurt raita rather than salted chutneys, as a full serving can contribute approximately 25–30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high glycemic load per serving (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mini naan is made from refined wheat flour (maida) and carries a high GI of 71 with a high glycemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to 1 mini naan piece, pair it with a protein-rich or high-fibre dip such as dal or raita, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter or ghee (~2.9 g per 100 g) and refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from ghee or butter used in naan preparation, combined with the high refined carbohydrate load, warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing a yogurt-based dip over cream-based sauces and limiting to 1–2 pieces per meal is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini Naan with Dip is an Indian-origin appetiser or snack dish consisting of small, individually portioned naan breads served alongside one or more dipping sauces. Naan is a leavened flatbread traditionally baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat, producing a soft, slightly chewy interior with characteristic charred blisters on the surface. The mini format reduces each piece to approximately 25–35 g, making it suitable for sharing or as a starter course. In Singapore restaurant contexts, the dip is typically mint chutney, yogurt raita, or a butter chicken sauce. The bread is made from refined wheat flour (maida), yogurt, yeast, salt, and butter or ghee, giving it a mildly tangy, rich flavour. Nutritionally, mini naan is a moderate-energy, high-carbohydrate food with a high glycemic index (GI ~71) due to its refined flour base. The dish provides moderate protein (~7 g per 100 g) and fat (~5.8 g per 100 g), with saturated fat from dairy. Sodium content is notable (~380 mg per 100 g) and increases with salted dips. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and glycemic load, and for those with hypertension due to sodium content. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable. Common in Indian restaurants across Singapore, it is often ordered as a starter or accompaniment to curries.
common_user_queries: What is mini naan with dip?
How many calories in mini naan?
What dip comes with mini naan?
How much sodium in naan bread?

confidence_reason: Naan bread macros are well-supported by ICMR-NIN data for plain naan. The composite dish (naan + dip) required weighted estimation as no single approved source provides nutrient data for 'mini naan with dip' as a combined item. GI is based on an analogous naan bread entry from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation and variability in dip type across restaurants.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan bread and is not reliably quantified for the dip component. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan bread. The dip component is assumed to be a yogurt-based raita or mint chutney at approximately 30% of total serving weight; if the dip is a cream-based butter chicken sauce, fat and saturated fat values would be higher. Restaurant-specific variation (Dugout - Circular Road, field survey data) may affect actual nutrient values. Price noted as SGD 16.00 per serving from field survey data source record.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Indian cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Derived composite per_100g nutrient values from ICMR-NIN (naan) and USDA FoodData Central (dip) with weighted estimation documented.
Applied GI of 71 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for naan bread as analogous estimate.
Documented -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg with explicit source-based justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to allowed values: closest_parent and none.
Confirmed gi_category = 'high' is correct for GI = 71 (>= 70 threshold).
Ensured all three health conditions present with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes with machine key type2_diabetes and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
